title: L04.6.comprehensions
body:
Toolkit for Finance and Financial Decision Making
Lecture 5.4: Comprehensions

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin
UNSW




                                                    1
 Comprehensions
 Comprehensions




 Comprehensions provide a concise way to construct list, dict & set containers.

      Comprehensions can be used in place of a for loop to populate standard containers.
      Comprehensions may make your code easier to read and faster than a corresponding loop.

                             There are no tuple comprehensions in Python.




                                                                                               2
 Comprehensions: List comprehensions
 Comprehensions: List comprehensions




    Many list-based loops can be translated into comprehensions

    A for loop that creates a list of perfect squares, x2 , where x is from 0 through 100.
1       squares = []
2       for x in range(100 + 1):
3           squares.append(x**2)




                                                                                             3
 Comprehensions: List comprehensions




    Many list-based loops can be translated into comprehensions

    A for loop that creates a list of perfect squares, x2 , where x is from 0 through 100.
1       squares = []
2       for x in range(100 + 1):
3           squares.append(x**2)



    . . . and its corresponding list comprehension.
1       squares = [x**2 for x in range(100 + 1)]




                                                                                             3
 Comprehensions: List comprehension syntax




 Lists may be constructed using a comprehension instead of a loop.


         1     [<expression> for <item> in <iterable>]


 The basic list comprehension syntax consists of the following:
      An opening square bracket [
      An <expression> that uses the <item> item object to produce a result that is placed in the
      list
      The for <item> in <iterable> indicating that each element of the <iterable> should be
      stored in the variable <item>
      A closing square bracket ]




                                                                                                   4
 Comprehensions: List comprehension syntax




 Lists comprehensions may optionally filter elements


         1     [<expression> for <item> in <iterable> if <condition>]


 The general list comprehension syntax consists of:
      An opening square bracket [
      An <expression> that uses the <item> item object to produce a result that is placed in the
      list
      The for <item> in <iterable> indicating that each element of the <iterable> should be
      stored in the variable <item>
      An if <condition> that provides a logical <condition> that must evaluate True for an
      element to be included in the resulting list
      A closing square bracket ]




                                                                                                   5
 Comprehensions: List comprehensions and loops




    Loops and list comprehensions are two methods to perform a tasks.

    A loop. . .
1       lst = []
2       for <item> in <iterable>:
3           if <condition>:
4                lst.append(<expressio>)

    . . . can be translated into a list comprehension.
1       lst = [<expression> for <item> in <iterable> if <condition>]


                          Similarly, a list comprehension can be translated into a loop.




                                                                                           6
 Comprehensions: Set and dictionary comprehensions
 Comprehensions: Set comprehensions syntax




 The syntax for set comprehensions is:


         1     {<expression> for <item> in <iterable> if <condition>}


 The set comprehension syntax consists of:
      An opening curly bracket {
      An <expression> that uses the <item> item object to produce a result that is placed in the
      set
      The for <item> in <iterable> indicating that each element of the <iterable> should be
      stored in the variable <item>
      An (optional) if <condition> that provides a logical <condition> that must evaluate True
      for an element to be included in the resulting set
      A closing curly bracket }




                                                                                                   7
 Comprehensions: Dictionary comprehensions syntax




 The syntax for dictionary comprehensions is:


         1     {<key_expr: value_expr> for <item> in <iterable> if <condition>}


 The dictionary comprehension syntax consists of:
      An opening curly bracket {
      A <key_expr: value_expr> expression indicating each key-value pair to place in the
      dictionary
          <key_expr> produces a key object from <item>. The key must be hashable.
          <value_expr> produces a value object from <item>
      The for <item> in <iterable> indicating that each element of the <iterable> should be
      stored in the variable <item>
      An (optional) if <condition> that provides a logical <condition> that must evaluate True
      for an element to be included in the resulting list
      A closing curly bracket }




                                                                                                 8
 Comprehensions: What about tuples?



 Python does not support tuple comprehensions.

 The following syntax will not produce a tuple:

         1     (<expression> for <item> in <iterable> if <condition>)

 Writing parentheses around a comprehension creates a generator expression, not a tuple.

         1     (x**2 for x in range(5))
         2     # <generator object ...>

 To get a tuple, you must pass the generator to the tuple constructor:

         1     tuple(x**2 for x in range(5))
         2     # (0, 1, 4, 9, 16)




 Generators will not be covered in this course, but it is important to know that ( ... ) creates a generator
                                            expression, not a tuple.




                                                                                                               9
 Comprehensions: Summary
 Comprehensions: Summary




 Comprehensions are very flexible!

      In this course, we will focus primarily on list and dict comprehensions.
      We will use standard looping structures when the corresponding comprehension requires
      complicated nesting or filtering conditions.
      The official Python Documentation is a good place to learn more about comprehensions:
          List comprehensions:
           https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html#list-comprehensions
          Dict comprehensions: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html#dictionaries
          Generator expressions (advanced):
           https://docs.python.org/3/reference/expressions.html#generator-expressions




                                                                                                      10
